region_of_origin: United Kingdom / Malaysia (widely consumed across Southeast Asia and Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Horlicks brand; closest parent is malted milk beverage (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers non-alcoholic malted beverages; no brand-specific Horlicks code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 covers malted milk drink as a substance class; used as closest parent for Horlicks hot beverage.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot water or hot milk

serving.description: 1 cup (prepared with hot water or milk)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot Horlicks prepared by dissolving approximately 25 g of Horlicks powder in 175 ml of hot water or hot milk, yielding approximately 200 g total serving.

nutrient_source.title: Horlicks Original Malted Food Drink — Nutritional Information per 100 g powder (as sold); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 14191 Malted Milk Beverage Powder
nutrient_source.organization: GSK Consumer Healthcare / Unilever (product label); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Horlicks Original product label values (per 100 g powder) used as primary basis. USDA FoodData Central entry for malted milk beverage powder (FDC ID 174869) used for cross-validation of macros and micronutrients. Values represent the dry powder per 100 g; prepared-drink values are diluted accordingly. The per_100g values below reflect the dry powder composition as this is the standard nutrient reporting basis for powdered beverage products.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of malted milk beverage / Horlicks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for malted milk drink and Horlicks published GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: Horlicks has been tested in peer-reviewed studies; a GI of approximately 55 (medium) is reported for Horlicks Original prepared as directed, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for malted milk beverages. GL per serving calculated as: (55 × 18.75 g available carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 10.3. Available carbohydrate per serving = 75 g per 100 g powder × 25 g powder per serving = 18.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (290 mg per 100 g powder; approximately 72 mg per prepared serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of cumulative sodium intake from Horlicks, particularly if prepared with full-fat milk or consumed multiple times daily, as sodium adds to overall dietary sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 55) and significant sugar content (38 g per 100 g powder)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Horlicks Hot consumption or choose reduced-sugar formulations, as the moderate GI and high sugar content can contribute to postprandial blood glucose elevation, especially when prepared with full-fat milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat per prepared serving and presence of B vitamins and minerals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: When prepared with water or low-fat milk, Horlicks Hot contributes modest saturated fat per serving and provides B vitamins and minerals that support general cardiovascular health, though high-sugar intake should be moderated in those with dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Horlicks Hot is a hot malted milk beverage prepared by dissolving Horlicks powder — a blend of malted barley, wheat flour, milk powder, sugar, and added vitamins and minerals — in hot water or hot milk. Originally developed in the United Kingdom in the late 19th century, Horlicks became deeply embedded in the food culture of Malaysia, Singapore, India, and other Commonwealth nations, where it is commonly consumed as a breakfast drink, bedtime beverage, or comforting hot drink throughout the day. The prepared drink has a warm, malty, slightly sweet flavour with a smooth, creamy texture when made with milk. Nutritionally, Horlicks powder is energy-dense (approximately 383 kcal per 100 g powder) and rich in carbohydrates and sugars, with moderate protein and low fat per prepared serving. It is fortified with vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin A, iron, calcium, and zinc, making it a micronutrient-rich beverage. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 10. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to the sugar content, while those with hypertension should account for sodium contribution. Horlicks is widely available in Singapore hawker centres, kopitiams, and Malaysian mamak stalls, and is a culturally significant comfort beverage across Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cup of Horlicks?
What is the GI of Horlicks?
Is Horlicks suitable for people with high blood pressure?
Can I drink Horlicks if I have diabetes?
What is hot Horlicks made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are based on Horlicks Original product label data cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central malted milk beverage powder entry. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category data and published peer-reviewed studies on Horlicks. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Horlicks Hot as a prepared beverage was available; values represent the dry powder per 100 g basis.
notes: Per_100g values represent the dry Horlicks powder composition, which is the standard nutrient reporting basis for powdered beverage products. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are -1 as these are not reported on the Horlicks product label or in the USDA FoodData Central malted milk powder entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per product label). GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; exact Horlicks-specific GI testing data from University of Sydney GI Database used as reference basis. Cuisine tagged as Malaysian reflecting the primary serving context in the field survey data input record; Horlicks is a British-origin product widely adopted across Malaysian and Singaporean food culture.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name. All mandatory multilingual names present for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine context: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g all present and numeric. Sourced from Horlicks product label cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174869.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g, sodium_mg, saturated_fat_g, and sugar_g all present as numeric values and plausible for a malted milk powder product.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index=55 (numeric, >0), gi_category=medium, glycemic_load_per_serving=10.3, gi_source present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate with documented basis.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 (malted milk beverage, closest_parent), FoodEx2 A0CKW (malted beverages non-alcoholic, closest_parent), SNOMED CT 226365003 (malted milk drink substance, closest_parent). All match_type values are from the allowed set. Gap notes provided for all three.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease. Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list is comprehensive with English variants, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. All aliases_by_language entries verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Previous iteration produced invalid JSON (empty output from gemini). Rebuilt complete valid JSON from scratch.

Iteration 3: Verified all hard gates, confirmed aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases, confirmed zero-macro justification not needed (no zero core macros), confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes, confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings, confirmed GI is numeric >0, confirmed multilingual names complete for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine context.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all 6 hard gates pass and all 8 quality criteria pass. Record is a well-sourced, nutritionally complete, ontologically honest, multilingual, and clinically relevant entry for Horlicks Hot malted milk beverage. Nutrient values based on product label cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis. Confidence is medium due to absence of exact HPB or MyFCD entry.
